one_line_takeaway: In the first large-scale (N=303) empirical study of Agent-Mediated Deception, only 8.6% of users noticed and just 2.7% correctly identified attacks launched through a compromised-but-faithfully-executing LLM agent, with domain experts and high-trust users proving paradoxically more vulnerable than novices.
key_finding: Only 8.6% of participants under the baseline condition (Guard 1) perceived anything unusual about the AMD attacks, and only 2.7% could accurately identify the underlying attack mechanism, despite 97.0% reporting high trust in AI agents and 75.3% feeling confident they could spot AI risks (an 'Illusion of Preparedness'). IT/technical domain experts were paradoxically more susceptible than general users in code-related scenarios (e.g., 0.0% vs 8.1% perception in CodeRepoAgent, 9.1% vs 13.5% in CodeEvalAgent), an 'Expert's Paradox' attributed to experts trusting the agent's process while scrutinizing only content. The authors identify six cascading cognitive failure modes (Task-Focused Tunneling, Partial Verification, Utility Override, Transparency Preference, Algorithmic Adherence, Vigilance Degradation) forming a 'cognitive escalation effect.' Multi-factor analysis found in-situ cognitive state (Consistency-checking and In-Situ Trust, both p<.0001) predicted perception far better than static traits like Initial Confidence, Trust, or Vigilance (all non-significant, p>.34), and higher education (M.S./Ph.D.) correlated with worse detection (4.64% vs 10.68%, p=.0028). Escalating guardrails improved outcomes monotonically -- risk perception 8.6%→20.0%→25.0% and accurate identification 2.7%→8.9%→17.2% across G1→G2→G3 -- but security warnings also paradoxically increased trust among users who missed the attack (the 'Transparency Preference' trap). Over 90% of users who did perceive an attack under G2/G3 reported increased future caution, supporting experiential learning ('security flight simulator') as an effective education strategy.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Agent-Mediated Deception (AMD) as a new threat framing in which an already-compromised LLM agent (via prompt injection, memory poisoning, or tool manipulation) is weaponized against the trusting human user who delegated a task to it, rather than the agent itself being the end target. To measure human susceptibility to AMD, the authors build HAT-Lab (Human-Agent Trust Laboratory), a high-fidelity behavioral-experiment platform with nine realistic task scenarios (e.g., HR-Agent, DoctorAgent, MailAgent, CodeRepoAgent, TripAgent) organized around a novel Trust Boundary Framework covering Perception, Memory, and Action. Using HAT-Lab they run the first large-scale empirical human-subjects study (N=303 Prolific participants) measuring how often users notice and correctly identify AMD attacks, what cognitive factors predict susceptibility, and how three escalating defense 'guardrails' (static disclaimer, persistent reminder, interactive alert) affect detection and downstream trust/behavior.
threat_model: Agent-Mediated Deception (AMD): the adversary does not attack the human directly (as in phishing/misinformation) but first compromises one of an agentic system's three core components -- Perception (via Indirect Prompt Injection, e.g., a hidden note in a resume or a poisoned email), Memory (via RAG/knowledge-base poisoning, state manipulation, or context bleeding across tasks), or Action (via tool-description hijacking that manipulates a tool's natural-language description so the agent's planner faithfully executes malicious logic, e.g., inflating a competitor's price). The compromised agent then executes the corrupted mission faithfully and correctly from its own standpoint, but this faithful execution is used to deceive the human user who trusts the agent's output/process. The user, not the agent, is the ultimate target, and the attack exploits the shift from passive suspicion (as with unsolicited phishing emails) to active, delegated trust in an agent the user has voluntarily tasked.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) the study is cross-sectional, providing a snapshot rather than longitudinal data on how trust dynamics and vigilance decay evolve over weeks/months; (2) the 'Expert's Paradox' is demonstrated only for IT/technical-background experts, leaving open whether it holds for experts in other high-stakes domains such as medicine, HR, or finance; (3) the nine attacks are statically configured rather than adaptive, so the study does not capture attacks that learn and exploit an individual user's specific trust patterns over time.

datasets_used: N=303 qualified Prolific participants (after data-quality exclusion)
9 HAT-Lab task scenarios spanning Perception (5), Memory (3), and Action (1) trust-boundary attacks (Table I)
Cross-model validation set of six mainstream LLM backends (e.g., GPT-4o, Claude 3.5) for stimulus reproducibility/consistency checks
quantitative_results: Risk Perception Rate by guardrail: G1 8.6% -> G2 20.0% -> G3 25.0%
Accurate Identification Rate by guardrail: G1 2.7% -> G2 8.9% -> G3 17.2%

Pre-survey: 97.0% trust AI to complete tasks accurately; 75.9% report checking AI outputs often; 75.3% feel confident perceiving AI risks; 96.4% report moderate-to-very-high confidence identifying risks; 28.4% report never experiencing AI issues
Domain experts vs general users perception: CodeRepoAgent 0.0% vs 8.1%; CodeEvalAgent 9.1% vs 13.5%; overall domain professionals 6.8% vs general users 9.6% (Guard 1)

Multi-factor predictors (Fisher's exact test): Consistency Low(<=2) 24.29% vs High(>=3) 6.91% perception, p<.0001; In-Situ Trust Low 23.19% vs High 7.02%, p<.0001; Education B.S./H.S. 10.68% vs M.S./Ph.D. 4.64%, p=.0028; Age 18-34 9.43% vs >=35 5.49%, p=.0307; Negative Experience Yes/Unsure 9.52% vs No 5.04%, p=.0112; Initial Confidence, Trust, Vigilance all non-significant (p=.39-.92)
Perceived vs Unperceived users (Table V): Trust 3.68 vs 3.96 (p=.007); Vigilance 4.31 vs 4.06 (p=.054); prior negative Experience 0.81 vs 0.65 (p=.007)
Proactive personal-judgment users: overall risk perception 19.6% vs baseline, a +39.5% relative lift; +51.2% lift in Guard 1 specifically
Platform validation: attack reproducibility >93.3% across models, 98.9% for GPT-4o; COMET cross-model consistency score 0.80; 100% rated tasks realistic; 78.6% rated system easy to use
DoctorAgent (highest-rated-severity scenario, 4.33/5) still had only 7.3% risk perception, i.e., >92% of attacks went undetected
Post-experience caution: over 90% of users who perceived an attack under Guard 2/Guard 3 reported increased future caution (Figure 10)
baselines_compared: No algorithmic/ML baselines -- this is a human-subjects study. Comparisons are between experimental conditions: Guard 1 (static disclaimer) as the weak-defense baseline vs Guard 2 (persistent reminder) vs Guard 3 (interactive alert)

Subgroup comparisons: domain professionals vs general users; users who perceived an attack vs those who did not; low vs high in-situ trust/consistency; users relying on proactive personal judgment vs not
